_patch_meta.description: Dimension review patch — C07: 127 dimension updates (anger, bitterness, envy, hatred, indignation, jealousy, wrath, zeal); Reg 205 = 0 groups; 6 Session B findings; 2 Session D pointers; 3 Volitional/Capacity corrections to programme vocabulary

record.finding: Anger governance is the paradigmatic inner-being governance challenge in Reg 4. Groups 79-004 (mastery of anger exceeds military conquest), 1554-001 (trembling restraint before God without sin), 38-001 (morally accountable anger under divine command), and 139-003 (wisdom counsel not to let anger kindle) show anger mastery as a distinct and critical sub-theme. Session B should map the vocabulary of anger-governance as a structured inner-being claim about will, character, and spiritual posture.

operations.description: Session B pointer — anger: governance as supreme inner-being act

record.finding: Two groups (1552-001: fierce anger co-existing with grief/loyalty; 37-003: righteous anger co-existing with sorrow at hardness of heart) show the inner being holding anger and love, or anger and grief, simultaneously as genuine compound affective states. Session B should examine whether Scripture treats compound anger-with-grief as a distinct inner-being mode and what theological conditions produce it.

operations.description: Session B pointer — anger: compound dual-affect states (anger with grief/love simultaneously)

record.finding: Bitterness in Reg 13 operates in two structural registers: (a) affective quality — bitter grief, anguish, affliction (something that happens to the inner person), and (b) dispositional-character state — root of bitterness, bitter jealousy as heart-state, bitter rebellion (something the inner person becomes). Session B should examine whether biblical bitterness is primarily an experience or a condition, and whether the two registers are sequential or independent.

operations.description: Session B pointer — bitterness: dual register (affective quality vs. settled dispositional condition)

record.finding: Reg 56 (envy) holds the full positive-negative dual register of the zeal/jealousy vocabulary. The same inner passion (consuming zealous desire) appears as: (a) destructive human jealousy/envy (Affective/Emotional, Moral/Conscience), (b) positive human zeal for God (Spiritual/God-ward), (c) divine jealousy as exclusive covenantal claim (Theological/Divine-Human). The inner capacity is identical; direction of passion determines valence. Session B should map this tripartite structure and ask what makes the same inner passion sanctified or destructive.

operations.description: Session B pointer — envy/zeal: tripartite inner-being structure

record.finding: The cho.mah (wall, H2346G) groups in Reg 178 (6784-001 to 6784-006) represent an XREF pattern where an architectural term's inner-being applications cover: misplaced trust (false security replacing God-trust), self-mastery (undefended city), purity (chaste self-possession), divine protection, divine attentiveness, and prophetic resilience. Session B should assess whether these six wall-groups illuminate a coherent inner-being pattern about security vs. vulnerability and self-possession vs. dissolution, and how this relates to the wrath registry.

operations.description: Session B pointer — wrath/cho.mah: wall metaphor applied to inner security, self-mastery, divine protection

record.finding: Reg 181 (zeal/spoudē) is uniformly positive — lacking the negative jealousy/envy register entirely. Dimension profile: Character/Disposition (3), Spiritual/God-ward (1), Volitional/Will (1). This contrasts with Reg 56 zeal vocabulary (predominantly Affective/Emotional and Spiritual/God-ward, both positive and negative valence). Session B should analyse what distinguishes earnest diligence (spoudē, Reg 181) from consuming passion (zēlos, Reg 56) — whether vocabulary difference, semantic field distinction, or theological claim about godly zeal.

operations.description: Session B pointer — zeal/spoudē: uniformly positive; contrast with zēlos dual register in Reg 56

record.description: parazēloō groups in Reg 96 show jealousy functioning simultaneously as human vulnerability and divine instrument: God provokes Israel's jealousy (939-001) to catalyse return, while Israel provokes God's jealousy (939-002) through idolatry. The same inner passion operates bidirectionally within the covenant relationship. Session D should examine jealousy as a structured relational dynamic within the divine-human covenant, relating Reg 96 (jealousy), Reg 56 (envy/zeal), and Reg 4 (anger).

operations.description: Session D pointer — jealousy as bidirectional covenant dynamic: Reg 96 × 56 × 4

record.description: Group 902-003 (sin.ah: hatred listed alongside love and envy as constitutive inner orientations of the person that cease at death) makes an anthropological claim: hatred is a fundamental inner capacity defining relational existence. Session D should examine what Scripture claims about the constitutive inner orientations of the human person — whether love, hatred, and envy form a structured set of relational capacities, and what their cessation at death implies for the inner being's post-mortem state.

operations.description: Session D pointer — hatred as constitutive inner capacity (alongside love and envy); cessation at death
